core_claim: RedSun is a file write local privilege escalation zero-day that allows attackers to drop a binary in System32 and execute as SYSTEM. Released by Nightmare Eclipse as part of the June 2026 vulnerability wave.

slides.on_slide_copy: REDSUN WRITES TO SYSTEM32. EXECUTES AS SYSTEM.
slides.subline: File write LPE. No CVE assigned yet. Nightmare Eclipse June wave exploit two.

slides.on_slide_copy: It bypasses TrustedInstaller to write any binary into a protected system folder.

slides.on_slide_copy: RedSun blends into normal installer activity because System32 writes are noisy.
slides.subline: Legitimate installers write there every day. Most endpoint tools ignore writes from the installer service, so detection baselines do not exist.

slides.on_slide_copy: No vendor patch exists, so defenders must catch this themselves.

caption:
Nightmare Eclipse released RedSun as the second exploit in their June 2026 wave. A file write local privilege escalation that drops a binary into System32 and executes it as SYSTEM.

The primitive abuses a junction/parse point in the Windows Installer service. The MSI installer runs as SYSTEM. The exploit redirects its trusted file copy operation to target System32. The binary lands. Execution follows via scheduled task or service registration.

No memory corruption. No race condition. Pure file write. Defenders assume System32 is immutable. It is not.

System32 file creation events (4663) are noisy. Legitimate installers write there daily. RedSun blends in. No CVE assigned yet. No patch timeline.

Defenders must baseline legitimate System32 writes: monitor for non-installer writes, block unsigned binaries in System32, use WDAC or AppLocker, hunt for MSI service spawning unusual children.

System32 is not immutable. It is a write target when a trusted service is misdirected.

Do you baseline System32 mutations in your environment?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[RedSun, Nightmare Eclipse, Windows zero-day, file write LPE, System32, Windows Installer, MSI service, WDAC, AppLocker, trusted service abuse]
comment_prompt: Do you baseline System32 mutations in your environment?
alt_text: Title card: RedSun writes to System32 and executes as SYSTEM. A file-write local privilege escalation with no CVE yet, the second exploit in Nightmare Eclipse's June wave. A System32 folder with a red binary slid inside and a SYSTEM token rising above it.

RedSun is the second exploit Nightmare Eclipse released in their June 2026 wave, targeting Windows with a direct file write into a protected system folder. No CVE is assigned and the exploit works on current Patch Tuesday builds.

RedSun bypasses TrustedInstaller ownership to place any binary into a protected system folder and run it as SYSTEM, with no memory corruption and no race condition involved. One file glows red among thousands of identical white system files in the dark.

The exploit abuses a junction point in the Windows Installer service: because that service runs as SYSTEM, it redirects its trusted file copy into the protected folder and the binary lands. A light arrow bends mid-air toward a guarded core.

RedSun blends into normal installer activity because writes to that folder happen every day from legitimate software. Endpoint tools ignore installer service file operations, leaving no detection baseline. A field of identical slabs with one pulsing red and otherwise indistinguishable.

No vendor patch exists yet, so defenders must baseline installer write patterns, block unsigned binaries in protected folders, enforce Windows Defender Application Control or AppLocker, and hunt the installer service for unusual child processes. A split scene: a signed binary behind a shield on one side, an unsigned one breaking it on the other.
System32 is not immutable. It is a write target when a trusted service is misdirected. A System32 folder icon, one half shielded and intact, the other fractured with a SYSTEM token escaping.
The call to action asks whether you baseline System32 mutations in your environment. A single pulse of light on a clean dark end card.

narration.text: A researcher called Nightmare Eclipse just dropped a privilege escalation exploit for Windows that writes a binary straight into the System32 folder and runs it as SYSTEM.
This is the second in a six-exploit wave released in June 2026, with no CVE assigned and no patch from Microsoft at time of release.

The technique abuses a junction point in the Windows Installer service: because that service runs as SYSTEM, redirecting its file copy operation drops an attacker-controlled binary into a protected folder without any memory corruption or race condition.

What makes this hard to catch is that legitimate installers write to System32 every single day, so the malicious write is noise inside noise, and most endpoint tools do not alert on installer service file operations.

Until a patch ships, defenders need to baseline what normal installer writes look like, block unsigned binaries from landing in that folder, enforce application control policies, and monitor the installer service for unusual child processes.
The assumption that System32 is off-limits to unprivileged attackers is exactly the gap this exploit lives in.
Have you ever basellined installer writes in your environment? Drop your answer in the comments.

beats.caption: Six-exploit wave. No CVE. Patched builds hit.
MSI service junction. SYSTEM copy redirected.
No memory corruption. File write. Binary lands.
System32 noise. Legit installers daily. Blends in.
Baseline mutations. Unsigned block. WDAC. Hunt MSI.
System32 not immutable. Trusted service misdirect.
